## Selfie

**Group ID:** `selfie`

| Reason                   | Reason ID         | Associated rejection label       | Description                                                                                                         |
| ------------------------ | ----------------- | -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Compare with ID mismatch | badFaceComparison | BAD\_FACE\_MATCHING, BAD\_SELFIE | The applicant face on the selfie is not clearly visible and cannot be matched against the photo in the ID document. |
| Liveness with watermark  | livenessWatermark | BAD\_SELFIE                      | There is camera text or a watermark on the Liveness record originating from the proprietary software.               |
| Selfie Liveness          | selfieLiveness    | BAD\_SELFIE                      | The applicant has failed the Liveness check.                                                                        |
| Selfie with ID           | selfieWithId      | BAD\_SELFIE                      | The applicant uploaded a selfie without the ID document. Selfie with the appropriate ID has been requested.         |
| Video Selfie             | videoSelfie       | BAD\_VIDEO\_SELFIE               | The applicant has failed to pass the video selfie check. A new video selfie has been requested.                     |
| Webcam selfie            | webcamSelfie      | BAD\_SELFIE                      | Applicant selfie has been requested.                                                                                |

## Unsuitable document

**Group ID:** `kybUnsuitableDocument`

| Reason                    | Reason ID                  | Associated rejection label  | Description                                                                                                                                        |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------- | --------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Black and white           | kybBlackAndWhite           | BLACK\_AND\_WHITE           | The applicant provided the document in black and white. A new image of the document has been requested.                                            |
| Data mismatch             | kybDataMismatch            | COMPANY\_DATA\_MISMATCH     | The data in the provided document does not match the data provided by the applicant.                                                               |
| Document template         | kybDocumentTemplate        | DOCUMENT\_TEMPLATE          | The provided document is a template.                                                                                                               |
| Expiration date           | kybExpirationDate          | EXPIRATION\_DATE            | The document has an unsuitable expiration date.                                                                                                    |
| Forgery                   | kybForgery                 | FORGERY                     | The provided document is suspected of being a forgery.                                                                                             |
| Graphics editing software | kybGraphicsEditingSoftware | GRAPHIC\_EDITOR             | The provided document has graphic modifications or edits applied. A document that has not been changed in any way should be uploaded.              |
| Incomplete document       | kybIncompleteDocument      | INCOMPLETE\_DOCUMENT        | The provided file does not include all pages and sides of the document.                                                                            |
| Low quality               | kybLowQuality              | LOW\_QUALITY                | The provided document file quality is too low to accept for verification.                                                                          |
| Missing translation       | kybMissingTranslation      | UNSUITABLE\_DOCUMENT        | There is no English translation applied to the document.                                                                                           |
| No date                   | kybNoDate                  | INCOMPLETE\_DOCUMENT        | There is no date specified in the document.                                                                                                        |
| Non-apostilled document   | kybNonApostilledDocument   | UNSUITABLE\_DOCUMENT        | The provided document has not been apostilled.                                                                                                     |
| Non-authentic document    | kybNonAuthenticDocument    | UNFILLED\_ID                | The provided document is not authentic.                                                                                                            |
| Non-certified document    | kybNonCertifiedDocument    | UNSUITABLE\_DOCUMENT        | The provided document is not a valid certified copy.                                                                                               |
| Non-certified translation | kybNonCertifiedTranslation | UNSUITABLE\_DOCUMENT        | The translation of the document has not been certified.                                                                                            |
| Non-notarized document    | kybNonNotarizedDocument    | UNSUITABLE\_DOCUMENT        | The provided document has not been notarized.                                                                                                      |
| Not a document            | kybNotADocument            | NOT\_DOCUMENT               | The provided file is not a document.                                                                                                               |
| Not readable document     | kybCouldntReadFields       | UNFILLED\_ID                | The company name or registration number is missing in the document.                                                                                |
| Not the latest version    | kybLastVersion             | OUTDATED\_DOCUMENT\_VERSION | The provided document is not the most recent version based on the state company register.                                                          |
| Personal document         | kybPersonalDocument        | UNSUITABLE\_DOCUMENT        | The provided file is a personal document that cannot be accepted for business verification.                                                        |
| Screenshot                | kybScreenshot              | SCREENSHOTS                 | The provided document is a screenshot. Upload your document as a scanned copy or PDF file.                                                         |
| Self-issued document      | kybSelfIssuedDocument      | UNSUITABLE\_DOCUMENT        | The provided document has been issued not by the state company/authority. A document that was issued and registered officially should be uploaded. |
| Unsealed document         | kybUnsealedDocument        | UNSUITABLE\_DOCUMENT        | The provided document has not been sealed.                                                                                                         |
| Unsigned document         | kybUnsignedDocument        | UNSUITABLE\_DOCUMENT        | The provided document has not been duly signed.                                                                                                    |

## Videoident

**Group ID:** `videoIdent`

| Reason                    | Reason ID                     | Associated rejection label        | Description                                                                     |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Applicant can not be seen | notSeen                       | UNSUITABLE\_ENV                   | On the Video Ident call, the applicant was not visible.                         |
| Bad connection            | badConnection                 | CONNECTION\_INTERRUPTED           | The Video Ident call connection was interrupted.                                |
| Interrupted interview     | applicantInterruptedInterview | APPLICANT\_INTERRUPTED\_INTERVIEW | On the Video Ident call, the applicant did not finish the interview.            |
| No docs                   | noSuitableDocs                | DOCUMENT\_MISSING                 | The applicant failed to provide the required documents on the Video Ident call. |
| Not alone                 | notAlone                      | UNSUITABLE\_ENV                   | On the Video Ident call, the applicant was not alone.                           |

## Wallet signature mismatch

**Group ID:** `walletSignatureMismatch`

| Reason                    | Reason ID               | Associated rejection label | Description                                  |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------- | -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Wallet Signature mismatch | walletSignatureMismatch | BAD\_PROOF\_OF\_PAYMENT    | Signature of the wallet has been mismatched. |


# Sumsub Token Sharing
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/sumsub-token-sharing

If you already use Sumsub for compliance, we can simplify some of our application flows by using their  system. Here's how it works:

<Info>
  Sumsub token sharing is only available for Consumer Card Programs.
</Info>

Sumsub token sharing works in both sandbox and production. You will need to do this flow twice, once for sandbox and once for production.

<Warning>
  Your Sumsub environment must match Rain's environment. Tokens generated from a **production** Sumsub instance will not work in Rain's **sandbox**, and vice versa. Ensure you are using the correct Sumsub environment for the Rain environment you are integrating with.
</Warning>

<Steps>
  <Step title="Request a Donor Key">
    Contact Rain to request a donor key for the desired environment (sandbox or production).
    Sandbox and production environments require separate donor keys. Request a sandbox key first to test the integration, then request a production key when you are ready to go live.
    The key will look something like `eyJ...`.
  </Step>

  <Step title="Switch Your Sumsub Instance to the Desired Environment (Sandbox or Production)">
    In your Sumsub dashboard, toggle the environment from Production to Sandbox. A yellow banner indicating "You're in Sandbox mode" appears when sandbox is active. Remember the key needs to match the environment.

    <Frame>
      <img alt="Sumsub sandbox mode banner" />
    </Frame>
  </Step>

  <Step title="Add Rain as a Recipient">
    1. Navigate to **Reusable Identity > Partners > Recipients** in your Sumsub dashboard
    2. Click **Add recipient**
    3. Enter the donor key that Rain provided
    4. Click **Continue** to complete the setup

    <Frame>
      <img alt="Sumsub Recipients page" />
    </Frame>

    <Frame>
      <img alt="Add recipient dialog" />
    </Frame>
  </Step>

  <Step title="Turn on Required Fields in Your Sumsub Instance">
    Your Sumsub instance must be configured to collect the following fields for Rain to process the consumer application.

    | Sumsub Fields         | Description                                                      |
    | --------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
    | `firstName`           | The person's first name                                          |
    | `lastName`            | The person's last name                                           |
    | `birthDate`           | The person's birth date                                          |
    | `tin`                 | The person's national ID (9-digit SSN if country of issue is US) |
    | `countryOfIssue`      | The country that issued the national ID                          |
    | `email`               | The person's email address                                       |
    | `phoneCountryCode`    | The user's phone country code (e.g., `1` for US)                 |
    | `phoneNumber`         | The user's phone number                                          |
    | `address.line1`       | Address line 1                                                   |
    | `address.city`        | City                                                             |
    | `address.region`      | State or region                                                  |
    | `address.postalCode`  | Postal code                                                      |
    | `address.countryCode` | Country code (ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format)                         |

    <Info>
      Enable all required fields in your Sumsub applicant data settings before generating share tokens. Missing fields will cause application creation to fail.
    </Info>
  </Step>

  <Step title="Generate a share token">
    A share token will look something like `_act-sbx-j...` You can generate a share token in two ways:

    * Use the Sumsub endpoint to [generate a share token](https://docs.sumsub.com/reference/generate-share-token). You will need to pass in our client id `raincards.xyz_43942` in sumsub's `forClientId` field.
    * Generate it directly from the sumsub website

      1. Open a production or sandbox applicant in Sumsub
      2. Click the three-dot menu (⋮) in the top-right corner
      3. Select **Generate share token**
      4. Click **Generate** to create the token
      5. Copy the token.

      <Frame>
        <img alt="Generate share token menu option" />
      </Frame>

      <Frame>
        <img alt="Generated share token dialog" />
      </Frame>
  </Step>

  <Step title="Use the share token in your application">
    When creating a [consumer application](/reference/applications/create-a-consumer-application-for-a-user), use the share token you generated in Step 5 and pass it in through our `sumsubShareToken` field. You will still need to submit other required fields, such as the user's occupation, salary, and phone number, but most of the user fields can be omitted. You can check all the required fields on the [endpoint reference page](/reference/applications/create-a-consumer-application-for-a-user).
  </Step>
</Steps>

### Troubleshooting

If you encounter the error **"Token is invalid, make sure you generate a new one each time, because the old ones are invalidated upon usage"**, check for these common causes:

| Issue                   | Solution                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| ----------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Token already used      | Share tokens can only be used once. Generate a new token for each application submission.                                                                                                                         |
| Wrong environment       | Make sure you are in the desired environment. If testing in sandbox, switch the toggle from Production to Sandbox in your Sumsub dashboard. You should see the yellow "You're in Sandbox mode" banner at the top. |
| Missing required fields | Ensure all required fields are filled in the Sumsub application before generating the token.                                                                                                                      |


# Supported Chains and Tokens
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/supported-chains-and-tokens

Rain is currently live on the following chains with support for the following tokens

|                     | USDC | USDT                     |
| ------------------- | ---- | ------------------------ |
| **Polygon**         | ✅    | ✅                        |
| **Base**            | ✅    | ✅                        |
| **Celo**            | ✅    | ✅                        |
| **Optimism**        | ✅    | ✅                        |
| **BNB Smart Chain** | ✅    | ❌                        |
| **Avalanche**       | ✅    | ✅                        |
| **Arbitrum**        | ✅    | ✅                        |
| **ZKsync**          | ✅    | ✅                        |
| **Solana**          | ✅    | ✅                        |
| **Plasma**          | ❌    | ✅ - Early access testing |
| **Stellar**         | ✅    | Coming later             |

This document provides a comprehensive mapping of chain IDs to their decimal equivalents, organized by blockchain type.

## EVM Chains

| Chain           | Dev/Testnet        | Prod/Mainnet |
| --------------- | ------------------ | ------------ |
| Polygon         | 80002 (Amoy)       | 137          |
| Celo            | 44787 (Alfajores)  | 42220        |
| Optimism        | 11155420 (Sepolia) | 10           |
| Arbitrum        | 421614 (Sepolia)   | 42161        |
| Avalanche       | 43113 (Fuji)       | 43114        |
| Base            | 84532 (Sepolia)    | 8453         |
| zkSync          | 300 (Sepolia)      | 324          |
| BNB Smart Chain | 97 (Testnet)       | 56           |
| Plasma          | 9746 (Testnet)     | 9745         |

| ------ | ------------ | ------------ |

| Chain   | Dev/Testnet    | Prod/Mainnet |
| ------- | -------------- | ------------ |
| Stellar | 1501 (Testnet) | 1500         |

# Testing Transactions in Sandbox
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/testing-transactions-in-sandbox



## Requirements

`Approved User` - You will need an approved user to test transactions. If you don't have one, follow the steps in [Signing Up A User](/docs/signing-up-a-customer).

`Collateral Contract` - For Partner-managed tenants, you have a single collateral smart contract. For Rain-Managed tenants, each user will have a collateral smart contract. Ensure you've funded your/your user's collateral contract otherwise, all your test transactions will be declined with `account_credit_limit_exceeded` reason.

`Card` - You will need to create a card tied to an approved user.

`Webhook URL` - this can be set on the Webhooks tab on your dashboard. Once configured, Rain will send webhook events to the specified URL.

## Step 1: Go to Transactions Section on the Rain Dashboard

* Navigate to transactions tab on your [Rain Dashboard](https://use-dev.raincards.xyz/issuing/transactions).

<img alt="" />

## Step 2: Create Test Transaction

* Select a card from the Spender dropdown. You can search by the last 4 digits of the card you created.
* Fill in the remaining required fields including `amount`, `Merchant Name`, and `Merchant Category` etc.
* Then, click the `Create Test Transaction` button.

<img alt="" />

After completing this step, you should see a new transaction entry displayed on the Transactions table. Depending on your webhook configuration, the new transaction could either have a status of `Declined` or `Pending`.

## Step 3: Simulate transaction settlement.

* Click the settlement icon on the transaction item you'd like to settle.

<img alt="" />

* You are able to simulate different Settlement scenarios listed in the [transaction flows.](/docs/settlement)

<img alt="" />

* Note that in production, this step is initiated by the merchant where they send all their sales data to Visa for clearing and settlement.

## Step 4: Review Webhooks

* On the webhooks tab, you should see the different webhook events that was triggered for the test transaction. Please see sample webhooks [here](/docs/sample-webhooks).

## Testing Zero-Amount Authorizations

You can simulate zero-amount authorization updates to test balance verification or PIN verification scenarios. These types of authorizations are common in card processing when merchants need to verify a card without charging any amount.

To test a zero-amount authorization:

1. Create a test transaction using the steps above with any amount.
2. Update the transaction amount to `0` using the transaction update feature.

This simulates scenarios where a merchant verifies a card is valid without placing a hold on funds.


# transaction
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/transaction

The webhooks covered here are triggered for all spend, collateral, payment, or fee-related transactions, notifying your systems as transactions move through their lifecycle states of requested, created, updated, and completed. These notifications allow you to integrate transaction events into your systems in real-time, enabling automated reconciliation, custom notifications, and seamless workflow integration without requiring manual polling of our API.

<Info>
  Spend transaction webhooks include an optional `eventReceivedAt` timestamp field. This field captures when Rain first received the event from the payment processor, which can help with debugging timing issues and building audit trails.

  By default, this field is excluded from webhook payloads. Contact your Rain account manager to enable it for your tenant.
</Info>

## `requested`

This webhook is sent when a user attempts to authorize a transaction, such as when making a purchase.

### `spend`

The `spend` webhook is triggered when a cardholder spends their card. [Partner-managed programs](https://docs.raincards.xyz/v2.0.0/docs/authorization-partner-managed#/) need to [put a hold on the collateral balance](https://docs.raincards.xyz/v2.0.0/docs/authorization-partner-managed#/approving-authorizations) and to respond to this webhook to authorize the transaction. You do not need to persist this information.

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  {
    id: string;  // webhookId
    resource: "transaction";
    action: "requested";
    eventReceivedAt?: string; // ISO 8601 timestamp
    body: {
      id?: string; // transactionId
      type: "spend";
      spend: {
        amount: number;
        currency: string;
        localAmount?: number;
        localCurrency?: string;
        authorizedAmount: number;
        previouslyAuthorizedAmount: number;
        merchantName: string;
        merchantId?: string;
        merchantCity: string;
        merchantCountry: string;
        merchantCategory: string;
        merchantCategoryCode: string;
        cardId: string;
        cardType: "physical" | "virtual";
        companyId?: string;
        userId: string;
        userFirstName: string;
        userLastName: string;
        userEmail: string;
        status: "pending";
        authorizedAt?: Date;
      };
    };
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

<Info>
  ### Key fields

  There are two different `id` fields. The one in the root is the `webhookId`, while the one on the `body` is the `transactionId`.
</Info>

### Response

<Warning>
  ### Limited availability

  Only Partner-managed programs can authorize or reject transactions. Rain-managed programs do not support this feature at this time.
</Warning>

* Respond with `200 OK` to approve the transaction.
* Respond with `401` or any other `4XX` status codes to reject it.

## `created`

This webhook is sent whenever a transaction is created.

### `collateral`

The `collateral` webhook is sent when collateral is added to a user or company contract. Since these transactions are final by default, no additional `completed` webhook will be sent.

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  {
    id: string;  // webhookId
    resource: "transaction";
    action: "created";
    body: {
      id: string; // transactionId
      type: "collateral";
      collateral: {
        amount: number;
        currency: string;
        chainId: number;
        walletAddress: string;
        transactionHash: string;
        companyId?: string;
        userId?: string;
        postedAt: Date;
      };
    };
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

<Info>
  ### Key fields

  There are two different `id` fields. The one in the root is the `webhookId`, while the one on the `body` is the `transactionId`.
</Info>

### `spend`

The `spend` webhook is sent when a transaction is created, whether it has been authorized or declined. You must persist this information.

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  {
    id: string;  // webhookId
    resource: "transaction";
    action: "created";
    eventReceivedAt?: string; // ISO 8601 timestamp
    body: {
      id: string; // transactionId
      type: "spend";
      spend: {
        amount: number;
        currency: string;
        localAmount?: number;
        localCurrency?: string;
        authorizedAmount?: number;
        authorizationMethod?: string;
        merchantName: string;
        merchantId?: string;
        merchantCity: string;
        merchantCountry: string;
        merchantCategory: string;
        merchantCategoryCode: string;
        cardId: string;
        cardType: "physical" | "virtual";
        companyId?: string;
        userId: string;
        userFirstName: string;
        userLastName: string;
        userEmail: string;
        status: "pending" | "declined";
        declinedReason?: string;
        authorizedAt?: Date;
      };
    };
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

<Info>
  ### Key fields

  There are two different `id` fields. The one in the root is the `webhookId`, while the one on the `body` is the `transactionId`.
</Info>

### `payment`

The `payment` webhook is sent when a payment is initiated on-chain. A separate webhook is sent when the payment is completed.

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  {
    id: string;  // webhookId
    resource: "transaction";
    action: "created";
    body: {
      id: string; // transactionId
      type: "payment";
      payment: {
        amount: number;
        currency: string;
        chainId: number;
        walletAddress: string;
        transactionHash: string;
        companyId?: string;
        userId?: string;
        status: "pending";
      };
    };
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

<Info>
  ### Key fields

  There are two different `id` fields. The one in the root is the `webhookId`, while the one on the `body` is the `transactionId`.
</Info>

### `transfer`

The `transfer` webhook is sent when a transfer is created via an automation (onramp or offramp). A separate webhook is sent when the transfer is completed.

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  {
    id: string;  // webhookId
    resource: "transaction";
    action: "created";
    body: {
      id: string; // transactionId
      type: "transfer";
      transfer: {
        source: {
          amount: string;
          currency: string;
          rail: string;
          referenceId?: string;
          address?: {
            type: "onchain" | "paymentAccount";
            address?: string;  // for onchain
            id?: string;       // for paymentAccount
          };
        };
        destination: {
          amount?: string;
          currency: string;
          rail: string;
          referenceId?: string;
          address?: {
            type: "onchain" | "paymentAccount";
            address?: string;  // for onchain
            id?: string;       // for paymentAccount
          };
        };
        depositAddress?: {
          type: "fiat" | "onchain";
          // fiat fields
          beneficiaryName?: string;
          beneficiaryAddress?: string;
          beneficiaryBankName?: string;
          beneficiaryBankAddress?: string;
          accountNumber?: string;
          routingNumber?: string;
          transferMessage?: string;
          // onchain fields
          address?: string;
        };
        exchangeRate?: string;
        status: "pending" | "awaiting_transfer" | "processing" | "settled" | "failed" | "cancelled" | "expired" | "refunded";
        createdAt: string;
        updatedAt: string;
      };
    };
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

<Info>
  ### Key fields

  There are two different `id` fields. The one in the root is the `webhookId`, while the one on the `body` is the `transactionId`.

  The `source` and `destination` `address` fields use a discriminated union based on the `type` field - either `onchain` with a wallet `address`, or `paymentAccount` with a payment account `id`.
</Info>

## `updated`

This webhook is sent whenever a transaction is updated. Note that the transaction may not have been created before this update.

### `spend`

This webhook is triggered for events such as incremental authorizations or refunds.

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  {
    id: string;  // webhookId
    resource: "transaction";
    action: "updated";
    eventReceivedAt?: string; // ISO 8601 timestamp
    body: {
      id: string; // transactionId
      type: "spend";
      spend: {
        amount: number;
        currency: string;
        localAmount?: number;
        localCurrency?: string;
        authorizedAmount?: number;
        authorizationUpdateAmount?: number;
        authorizationMethod?: string;
        merchantName: string;
        merchantId?: string;
        merchantCity: string;
        merchantCountry: string;
        merchantCategory: string;
        merchantCategoryCode: string;
        enrichedMerchantIcon?: string;
        enrichedMerchantName?: string;
        enrichedMerchantCategory?: string;
        cardId: string;
        cardType: "physical" | "virtual";
        companyId?: string;
        userId: string;
        userFirstName: string;
        userLastName: string;
        userEmail: string;
        status: "pending" | "reversed" | "declined";
        declinedReason?: string;
        authorizedAt: Date;
      };
    };
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

<Info>
  ### Key fields

  There are two different `id` fields. The one in the root is the `webhookId`, while the one on the `body` is the `transactionId`.
</Info>

## `completed`

This webhook is sent whenever a transaction reaches a final state. Note that the transaction may not have been created before this update.

### `spend`

The `spend` webhook is sent when a transaction is settled. No response is required.

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  {
    id: string;  // webhookId
    resource: "transaction";
    action: "completed";
    eventReceivedAt?: string; // ISO 8601 timestamp
    body: {
      id: string; // transactionId
      type: "spend";
      spend: {
        amount: number;
        currency: string;
        localAmount?: number;
        localCurrency?: string;
        authorizedAmount?: number;
        authorizationMethod?: string;
        merchantName: string;
        merchantId?: string;
        merchantCity: string;
        merchantCountry: string;
        merchantCategory: string;
        merchantCategoryCode: string;
        enrichedMerchantIcon?: string;
        enrichedMerchantName?: string;
        enrichedMerchantCategory?: string;
        cardId: string;
        cardType: "physical" | "virtual";
        companyId?: string;
        userId: string;
        userFirstName: string;
        userLastName: string;
        userEmail: string;
        status: "completed";
        authorizedAt: Date;
        postedAt: Date;
      };
    };
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

<Info>
  ### Key fields

  There are two different `id` fields. The one in the root is the `webhookId`, while the one on the `body` is the `transactionId`.

  The `localAmount` and `localCurrency` fields are populated when the transaction involves a foreign currency or when it is a standard settlement where the authorized amount equals the final settled amount. These fields may be `undefined` in certain scenarios, such as partial captures where the authorized and settled amounts differ.
</Info>

### `payment`

The `payment` webhook is sent when an initiated on-chain payment is completed.

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  {
    id: string;  // webhookId
    resource: "transaction";
    action: "completed";
    body: {
      id: string; // transactionId
      type: "payment";
      payment: {
        amount: number;
        currency: string;
        chainId: number;
        walletAddress: string;
        transactionHash: string;
        companyId?: string;
        userId?: string;
        status: "completed";
        postedAt: Date;
      };
    };
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

<Info>
  ### Key fields

  There are two different `id` fields. The one in the root is the `webhookId`, while the one on the `body` is the `transactionId`.
</Info>

### `transfer`

The `transfer` webhook is sent when a transfer initiated via an automation (onramp or offramp) is completed.

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  {
    id: string;  // webhookId
    resource: "transaction";
    action: "completed";
    body: {
      id: string; // transactionId
      type: "transfer";
      transfer: {
        source: {
          amount: string;
          currency: string;
          rail: string;
          referenceId?: string;
          address?: {
            type: "onchain" | "paymentAccount";
            address?: string;  // for onchain
            id?: string;       // for paymentAccount
          };
        };
        destination: {
          amount?: string;
          currency: string;
          rail: string;
          referenceId?: string;
          address?: {
            type: "onchain" | "paymentAccount";
            address?: string;  // for onchain
            id?: string;       // for paymentAccount
          };
        };
        depositAddress?: {
          type: "fiat" | "onchain";
          // fiat fields
          beneficiaryName?: string;
          beneficiaryAddress?: string;
          beneficiaryBankName?: string;
          beneficiaryBankAddress?: string;
          accountNumber?: string;
          routingNumber?: string;
          transferMessage?: string;
          // onchain fields
          address?: string;
        };
        exchangeRate?: string;
        status: "settled";
        createdAt: string;
        updatedAt: string;
      };
    };
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

<Info>
  ### Key fields

  There are two different `id` fields. The one in the root is the `webhookId`, while the one on the `body` is the `transactionId`.

  The `source` and `destination` `address` fields use a discriminated union based on the `type` field - either `onchain` with a wallet `address`, or `paymentAccount` with a payment account `id`.
</Info>


# Transaction Issues & Disputes
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/transaction-issues-disputes

This page covers how Rain manages unauthorized transactions, disputes, refunds, chargebacks, pending payments, in-progress authorizations, and terminal declines.

## Webhooks

Rain sends webhook notifications to track dispute lifecycle events in real-time. These webhooks are informational and do not require a response.

### `created`

<Warning>
  ### Dispute created webhook

  This webhook is sent when a new dispute is created for a transaction.
</Warning>

<CodeGroup>
  ```json json theme={null}
  {
    "id": "wh_1a2b3c4d5e6f",
    "resource": "dispute",
    "action": "created",
    "body": {
      "id": "dis_9z8y7x6w5v4u",
      "transactionId": "txn_4k3j2h1g0f9e",
      "status": "pending",
      "textEvidence": "Customer claims unauthorized transaction",
      "disputeAmount": 15000,
      "creditAmount": null,
      "createdAt": "2025-11-18T10:30:00Z"
    }
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

**Note**: The `textEvidence` and `creditAmount` fields are optional and may not be present in all dispute webhooks. The `disputeAmount` field contains the disputed amount in cents and must be less than or equal to the transaction amount. When creating a dispute, `disputeAmount` is optional—if omitted, it defaults to the full transaction amount. This allows partial disputes when you only want to dispute a portion of the total. The `creditAmount` field is only present when a dispute is accepted.

### `updated`

<Warning>
  ### Dispute status update webhook

  This webhook is triggered when a dispute's status changes.
</Warning>

Provides real-time updates on dispute resolution progress.

<CodeGroup>
  ```json json theme={null}
  {
    "id": "wh_2b3c4d5e6f7g",
    "resource": "dispute",
    "action": "updated",
    "body": {
      "id": "dis_9z8y7x6w5v4u",
      "transactionId": "txn_4k3j2h1g0f9e",
      "status": "accepted",
      "textEvidence": "Customer claims unauthorized transaction",
      "disputeAmount": 15000,
      "creditAmount": 15000,
      "createdAt": "2025-11-18T10:30:00Z",
      "resolvedAt": "2025-11-20T14:45:00Z"
    }
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

**Note**: The `textEvidence` and `creditAmount` fields are optional and may not be present in all dispute webhooks. The `disputeAmount` field contains the disputed amount in cents and must be less than or equal to the transaction amount. When creating a dispute, `disputeAmount` is optional—if omitted, it defaults to the full transaction amount. This allows partial disputes when you only want to dispute a portion of the total. The `creditAmount` field is only present when a dispute is accepted or resolved by merchant refund, and stores the credit amount in cents (e.g., `15000` = \$150.00). The `resolvedAt` field only appears when the dispute is resolved (status is `"canceled"`, `"accepted"`, `"rejected"`, or `"resolvedByMerchant"`).

**Possible status values**: `"pending"`, `"inReview"`, `"accepted"`, `"rejected"`, `"canceled"`, `"resolvedByMerchant"`

<Info>
  ### Key fields

  There are two different `id` fields. The one in the root is the `webhookId`, while the one in the `body` is the `disputeId`.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### How are disputes for unknown payments investigated?

  Investigations are conducted by the card network and may take up to 60 business days for resolution.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### How can I block a suspicious merchant?

  Tag **@RainSupport** in Slack and include:

  * The **merchant name**
  * The **reason** for the block request

  Rain’s team will review and take appropriate action.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### How do I initiate a dispute on a card transaction?

  Submit the following using Rain's [Dispute API](/reference/disputes/create-a-dispute-for-a-transaction):

  * Transaction ID
  * Transaction date
  * Amount
  * Dispute reason
  * Supporting evidence (e.g., documents, screenshots)
</Info>

<Info>
  ### How is a refund handled for a canceled order that's still pending?

  Refunds for canceled orders may take up to 30 business days once the pending status is cleared.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### How long do chargebacks take to resolve?

  Chargebacks typically resolve within 30–60 business days.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### How long does it take for a refund to be credited?

  Refunds generally complete within 30 business days after the merchant processes the transaction.

  If the refund is delayed beyond this period, escalate the issue to **Rain support**.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What are the associated fees for disputes?

  A \$30 chargeback fee applies to each submitted dispute.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What does it mean if a transaction is `pending`?

  A `pending` status indicates that the transaction is still under review or waiting for the merchant to finalize or cancel it.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What happens if a refund is declined?

  Declined refunds are not automatically credited back.

  The user must ask the merchant to retry the refund.

  If unresolved, escalate with **Rain support**.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What if a refund takes longer than 30 business days?

  If a refund exceeds 30 business days:

  1. Gather the **Transaction ID**.
  2. Provide the **last 4 digits of the card**.
  3. Escalate via **@RainSupport**.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What should I do if a merchant refund remains pending?

  If the status is pending for more than 10 days, it may fail.

  After 30 business days, escalate the issue to **Rain support**.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What should I do if a payment is not authorized by the processor?

  If there is no transaction ID, Rain did not receive the authorization request.

  Steps to troubleshoot:

  * Check with the merchant to see if there were any issues on their platform.
  * Try another payment method if the issue persists.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What should I do if a transaction is declined at the terminal but debited from the account?

  First, confirm with the merchant whether the transaction was declined on their system. If the authorization was never finalized, the funds will typically auto-return to the account without further action.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What should I do if a user reports an unauthorized payment?

  You should perform the following actions:

  1. Immediately cancel the affected card.
  2. Issue a new card to the user.
  3. File a dispute using the **Dispute API**.
</Info>

## Dispute Reimbursement Process

Rain automatically processes reimbursements when a dispute is accepted.

### Reimbursement timeline

1. Create a dispute using the [Dispute API](/reference/disputes/create-a-dispute-for-a-transaction)
2. Rain reviews the dispute (status: `"inReview"`)
3. If accepted (status: `"accepted"`), Rain processes the reimbursement
4. You receive a `transaction.completed` webhook for the credit transaction
5. The cardholder balance is updated with the credit amount

### What happens during reimbursement

When Rain processes an accepted dispute:

* A new transaction is created with a **negative amount** (credit to the account)
* The amount matches the `creditAmount` field from the `dispute.updated` webhook
* The transaction appears in transaction history as type `"spend"` with a negative amount
* The cardholder account balance is credited
* You receive a `transaction.completed` webhook for the credit

### Webhook notifications

You receive webhook notifications during the dispute lifecycle:

1. **`dispute.updated`** - Sent when the dispute status changes (including `"accepted"`, `"rejected"`, `"canceled"`, `"resolvedByMerchant"`, or `"inReview"`). The `creditAmount` field is only present when the status is `"accepted"` or `"resolvedByMerchant"`.
2. **`transaction.completed`** - Sent when Rain processes the reimbursement for an accepted dispute, includes the negative transaction amount

The `transaction.completed` webhook follows the same format as other transaction webhooks, with a negative `amount` field indicating a credit.

<Info>
  ### When a merchant processes a refund, is it immediately available to the user?

  * **Rain-managed Programs**: Refunds go to the user's card balance.
  * **Partner-managed Programs**: Refunds are credited to the reserve balance.

  **Tip**: Always wait for the refund to settle before acting to avoid potential fraud.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### Who handles transaction disputes and issues?

  Rain’s support team handles all disputes.

  It's important to notice that disputes must be initiated via Rain's [Dispute API](/reference/disputes/create-a-dispute-for-a-transaction) .
</Info>

<Info>
  ### Why does a payment stay pending for several days?

  Payments can remain pending for up to 30 business days. During this time, the funds are neither fully debited nor refunded.

  Once the merchant confirms or voids the transaction, the pending status will be resolved.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### Why is a transaction reversed?

  A transaction may reverse due to:

  * Merchant decline.
  * Terminal errors.

  If the reversal is not posted within 30 days, escalate the case via **@RainSupport**.
</Info>


# Transaction Lifecycle
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/transaction-lifecycle

Transactions typically occur in two phases:

<Card title="Authorization" href="/docs/authorization">
  Learn more about authorization flow for Rain-managed and Partner-managed programs.
</Card>

<Card title="Settlement" href="/docs/settlement">
  Learn more about settlement flow for Rain-managed and Partner-managed programs.
</Card>


# Transfer Collateral Accounts
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/transfer-collateral-accounts

If a user needs to update their wallet address used to manage a collateral account, they can follow the process below to update the admin addresses.

# Solana

### [Code example can be found here](https://github.com/SignifyHQ/collateral-contract-integration-examples/tree/main/src/solana/examples/collateralTeamTransfer)

The general flow of the Collateral transfer\_team flow is described as follows:

<img alt="" />

Where:

* **Admin**: Is the admin of the Collateral account that wants to transfer it to either a new admin or group of admins.
* **Admin Signer**: is the Solana account set as admin into the Collateral account that you must have access to sign both transactions and Buffers of information. It can be a custodial wallet like Phantom Wallet, a non-custodial like Privy, a smart account or a multi-sign account.
* **Message Generator**: Is a utility included in this example at the file `messageGenerator.ts` that helps to generate the message that must be signed by your **Admin Signer** to authorize the transference of your Collateral.
* **Solana RPC**: A Solana node connected to the network on either Mainnet, Devnet or Localnet.
* **Collateral**: Is the Collateral account stored in the Solana network that you want to transfer.
* **Collateral Admin Signatures**: Is a support account that will helps to temporary store the autorization given by you **Admin Signer** to transfer the **Collateral** account.

This flow is implemented under the example at the file `index.ts` using a Keypair of the **Admin Signer** to sign the authorization message and send the transaction to the chain. If you want to go deep into the example jump to the [What This Script Does section](https://github.com/SignifyHQ/collateral-contract-integration-examples/tree/main/src/solana/examples/collateralTeamTransfer#-what-this-script-does). Otherwise, continue reading the [How to Create Your Own Integration](https://github.com/SignifyHQ/collateral-contract-integration-examples/tree/main/src/solana/examples/collateralTeamTransfer#-how-to-create-your-own-integration) section.

## 🔧 How to Create Your Own Integration

This section provides a step-by-step guide for creating your own integration to transfer collateral team ownership. It is assumed that you have your own way to load the signer in your environment.

### Step 1: Generate the Request

First, import the types and create a `TransferCollateralTeamRequest` with the new information for the collateral account:

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  import { TransferCollateralTeamRequest } from "./types";
  import { PublicKey } from "@solana/web3.js";

  // Create the transfer request
  const transferRequest: TransferCollateralTeamRequest = {
    newName: "New Team Name", // The new name for the collateral account
    newAdmins: [
      new PublicKey("NewAdmin1PublicKeyHere"),
      new PublicKey("NewAdmin2PublicKeyHere")
    ], // Array of new admin public keys
    newAdminThreshold: 2 // Number of admins required to approve future operations
  };
  ```
</CodeGroup>

### Step 2: Generate and Sign the Message

Import the message generator and create a `TransferCollateralTeamMessage` to generate the message that needs to be signed:

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  import { TransferCollateralTeamMessage } from "./messageGenerator";

  // Get the current admin data nonce from the collateral account
  const currentNonce = collateralAccount.adminDataNonce;

  // Create the message to sign
  const transferMessage = new TransferCollateralTeamMessage(transferRequest, currentNonce);

  // Generate the message buffer that needs to be signed
  const messageBuffer = Buffer.from(transferMessage.encode(), "hex");

  // Sign the message with your admin signer
  // Note: This is where you would use your own signer implementation
  const signature = await signer.signMessage(messageBuffer);
  ```
</CodeGroup>

### Step 3: Upload the Signatures

Create a transaction with two instructions to upload the signatures:

#### 3.1: Ed25519 Instruction

For collateral accounts with a single admin, use the standard `Ed25519Program`:

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  import { Ed25519Program } from "@solana/web3.js";

  // Create Ed25519 instruction for single admin
  const ed25519Instruction = Ed25519Program.createInstruction({
    publicKey: signer.publicKey.toBytes(),
    message: messageBuffer,
    signature: signature
  });
  ```
</CodeGroup>

For collateral accounts with multiple admins, use the extended program:

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  import { Ed25519ExtendedProgram } from "../utils/ed25519.program";

  // Create Ed25519 instruction for multiple admins
  const signatureData = {
    signer: signer.publicKey,
    signature: signature,
    message: messageBuffer
  };

  const ed25519Instruction = Ed25519ExtendedProgram.createSignatureVerificationInstruction([signatureData]);
  ```
</CodeGroup>

#### 3.2: Submit Signatures Instruction

Create the submit signatures instruction using the Rain program:

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  import { Program } from "@coral-xyz/anchor";
  import { Main } from "../types/main";

  // Create the signature submission request
  const signatureRequest = {
    targetNonce: currentNonce,
    signatureSubmissionType: {
      transferCollateralTeam: {
        "0": transferRequest
      }
    },
    salts: [Array.from(crypto.randomBytes(32))] // Random salt for each signature
  };

  // Create the submit signatures instruction
  const submitSignaturesIx = await program.methods
    .submitSignatures(signatureRequest as any)
    .accounts({
      rentPayer: feePayer.publicKey,
      collateral: collateralAddress,
      collateralAdminSignatures: signaturesAccountAddress,
    })
    .instruction();

  // Send the transaction with both instructions
  const transaction = new Transaction()
    .add(ed25519Instruction)
    .add(submitSignaturesIx);

  const txSignature = await sendAndConfirmTransaction(connection, transaction, [feePayer]);
  console.log("Signatures uploaded:", txSignature);
  ```
</CodeGroup>

### Step 4: Send the Transfer Team Transaction

Finally, invoke the `transfer_collateral_team` transaction using the Anchor program:

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  // Generate the PDA for the signatures account
  const signaturesAccountAddress = CollateralAdminSignatures.generateTransferCollateralTeamPDA(
    collateral,
    transferRequest,
    program.programId
  );

  // Create and send the transfer transaction
  const transferTx = await program.methods
    .transferCollateralTeam(transferRequest)
    .accounts({
      sender: feePayer.publicKey,
      collateral: collateralAddress,
      collateralAdminSignatures: signaturesAccountAddress,
    })
    .signers([feePayer])
    .rpc();

  console.log("Transfer completed:", transferTx);
  ```
</CodeGroup>

### Important Notes

* **Signer Implementation**: You must implement your own way to load and use the signer in your environment
* **Transaction Size Limits**: Solana has a 1232-byte transaction size limit, so keep the number of admins reasonable (recommended max: 5)
* **Nonce Management**: Always use the current adminDataNonce from the collateral account to prevent replay attacks
* **Error Handling**: Implement proper error handling for network issues, insufficient funds, and invalid signatures
* **Testing**: Always test on devnet before deploying to mainnet

## 🏗️ Design Considerations

### Why This Two-Step Flow?

**Authorization Mechanism**: The authorization is provided by admins signing a message that includes the operation arguments (new name, list of admins, and threshold value). This signature is submitted to the chain for verification and stored in an account that holds the authorization until consumed during transfer execution. Verification occurs by:

1. Checking that the signature generated using the Ed25519 curve belongs to a current admin of the collateral
2. Verifying that the signed message matches the expected one corresponding to the transfer invocation arguments

**Two-Step Signature Submission**: Signatures are submitted in two steps due to Solana's transaction size limitation of 1232 bytes. Each signature takes 160 bytes of transaction space for verification, which for some operations allows only 2 signatures per transaction. By submitting signatures to a storage account first, we unlock the admin limit by sending signatures in batches of 4-5 per transaction, then executing the transfer with the pre-verified signatures.

### [Ready to use example can be found here](https://github.com/SignifyHQ/collateral-contract-integration-examples/tree/main/src/solana/examples/collateralTeamTransfer#-how-to-create-your-own-integration)


# Ultimate Beneficial Owner (UBO)
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/ultimate-beneficial-owner-ubo



<Check>
  ### Corporate program only

  This page applies to Corporate Programs. Take note of this information if you are using a Corporate Program.
</Check>

In the Rain system, an Ultimate Beneficial Owner (UBO) is an individual who holds significant control over a company applying for a corporate card program. This concept is required under compliance rules and is essential to the company application process.

## UBO definition under compliance rules

A UBO is any individual who meets at least one of the following criteria:

* Owns 25% or more of a company’s shares.
* Controls 25% or more of a company’s voting rights.
* Exercises significant influence over the company in another way.

All companies have at least one UBO, but UBOs do not need to be Rain users. Instead, they are listed as part of the corporate application.

## UBOs in the Rain system

Identifying UBOs is a crucial step in the corporate application process, ensuring compliance and verification of company ownership. The following sections address key questions about UBOs and their role in the application process.

<Info>
  ### Is the UBO required in the corporate application?

  Yes. A company application cannot be fully approved until all UBOs have been processed.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### Is the UBO the same as the user created during the company application?

  No. The individual created in the application step is not necessarily the UBO. However, all UBOs must be added as representatives in the application.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What types of users exist in a corporate application?

  There are two distinct roles in a corporate application:

  1. **UBOs** – Individuals who own 25% or more of the company.
  2. **Representatives** – Users who are authorized to act on behalf of the company during the application.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### Can a company application be started without adding UBOs?

  Yes, but we do not recommend this. The company will not be fully approved until all required UBOs are added and verified.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### How many UBOs can a company have?

  There is no fixed limit. Depending on its ownership structure, a company may have one UBO or hundreds.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### Are UBOs necessary before issuing cards?

  Yes. UBOs must be processed and approved before the company can start issuing cards.
</Info>


# user
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/user



<Check>
  ### Consumer Programs only

  This page applies to Consumer Programs.
</Check>

<Info>
  User updated webhooks include an optional `eventReceivedAt` timestamp field. This field captures when Rain first received the compliance status update event, which can help with debugging timing issues and building audit trails.

  By default, this field is excluded from webhook payloads. Contact your Rain account manager to enable it for your tenant.
</Info>

## `updated`

This webhook is sent whenever a user's compliance status is updated. No response is required.

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  {
    id: string;  // webhookId
    resource: "user";
    action: "updated";
    version: "1.0.0";
    eventReceivedAt?: string; // ISO 8601 timestamp
    body: {
      id: string; // userId
      email: string;
      firstName: string;
      lastName: string;
      applicationStatus: "approved" | "pending" | "needsInformation" | "needsVerification" | "manualReview" | "denied" | "locked" | "canceled";
      applicationReason: string;
      applicationCompletionLink?: {
        url: string;
        params: Record<string, string>;
      };
    },
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

<Info>
  ### Key fields

  There are two different `id` fields. The one in the root is the `webhookId`, while the one on the `body` is the `userId`.
</Info>

<Info>
  The `applicationReason` field contains comma-separated rejection labels. See the [webhook rejection label reference](/docs/sumsub-temporary-rejection#webhook-rejection-label-reference) for a complete reference of all labels and their meanings.
</Info>


# User Balance
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/user-balance

The  API provides an overview for a specific user within a tenant. It retrieves key balance metrics, including the user's credit limit, pending charges, posted charges, balance due, and spending power. You can use these metrics for managing user accounts, tracking spending, and ensuring financial accountability.

### How Balances Are Calculated

The response from the API will provide these fields:

1. Credit Limit - Represents the maximum credit assigned to the user.
2. Pending Charges - Represents transactions that are authorized but not yet finalized.
3. Posted Charges - Represents transactions that have been finalized or settled.
4. Balance Due - Represents the total amount owed by the user.
5. Spending Power - Represents the remaining credit available to the user.


# Using Encryption Outside of a Browser Environment
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/using-encryption-outside-of-a-browser-environment

Some of the code on the  guide may not work outside of a browser due to reliance on  and other browser APIs. Below are alternative implementations for server-side environments using Node.js.

<Warning>
  ### Security best practices

  * Never store decrypted card details.
  * Only request full card details when absolutely necessary.
  * Always use the latest encryption libraries to maintain security.
</Warning>

## Step 1: Generating the session ID

Use the `generateSessionID` method to generate the `SessionId`. This ensures that only the correct user can decrypt the data. You'll need the [public RSA key for your environment](/docs/resource-sessionid-keys):

### Requirements

* The secret must be a 32-character hexadecimal string with no spaces or dashes.
* Encryption must use RSA-OAEP padding with the provided public key.

### Example session ID generation

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  import crypto from "crypto";

  async function generateSessionId(pem, secret) {
    if (!pem) throw new Error("pem is required");
    if (secret && !/^[0-9A-Fa-f]+$/.test(secret)) {
      throw new Error("secret must be a hex string");
    }

    const secretKey = secret ?? crypto.randomUUID().replace(/-/g, "");
    const secretKeyBase64 = Buffer.from(secretKey, "hex").toString("base64");
    const secretKeyBase64Buffer = Buffer.from(secretKeyBase64, "utf-8");
    const secretKeyBase64BufferEncrypted = crypto.publicEncrypt(
      {
        key: pem,
        padding: crypto.constants.RSA_PKCS1_OAEP_PADDING,
        oaepHash: 'sha1'
      },
      secretKeyBase64Buffer,
    );

    return {
      secretKey,
      sessionId: secretKeyBase64BufferEncrypted.toString("base64"),
    };
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

* The `sessionId` is required to make an API request.
* The `secretKey` will be needed for decryption later.

## Step 2: Sending the API request

Once the `sessionId` is generated, you can send a request to retrieve encrypted card details.

### Requesting encrypted card details

Send a request to the [get a card's encrypted data](/reference/cards/get-a-cards-encrypted-data) endpoint.

### Example API request

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  curl --request GET \
       --url https://api-dev.raincards.xyz/v1/issuing/cards/cardId/secrets \
       --header 'SessionId: sessionId' \
       --header 'accept: application/json'
  ```
</CodeGroup>

### Example API response

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  {
    "encryptedPan": {
      "iv": "base64_iv_string",
      "data": "base64_encrypted_pan"
    },
    "encryptedCvc": {
      "iv": "base64_iv_string",
      "data": "base64_encrypted_cvc"
    }
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

## Step 3: Decrypting the encrypted card data

To decrypt the received encrypted card details, use AES-128-GCM decryption.

### Example card data decryption

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  import crypto from "crypto";

  async function decryptSecret(base64Secret, base64Iv, secretKey) {
    if (!base64Secret) throw new Error("base64Secret is required");
    if (!base64Iv) throw new Error("base64Iv is required");
    if (!secretKey || !/^[0-9A-Fa-f]+$/.test(secretKey)) {
      throw new Error("secretKey must be a hex string");
    }

    const secret = Buffer.from(base64Secret, "base64");
    const iv = Buffer.from(base64Iv, "base64");
    const secretKeyBuffer = Buffer.from(secretKey, "hex");
    
  	 // AES-GCM typically uses a 128-bit (16-byte) authentication tag
    const tagLength = 16;

    // Separate the ciphertext from the authentication tag
    const ciphertext = secret.subarray(0, -tagLength);
    const authTag = secret.subarray(-tagLength);

    const cryptoKey = crypto.createDecipheriv("aes-128-gcm", secretKeyBuffer, iv);
    cryptoKey.setAutoPadding(false);

    const decrypted = cryptoKey.update(secret);
    
    return decrypted.toString("utf-8").trim();
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>

### Example final output

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  const decryptedCardNumber = await decryptSecret(data.encryptedPan.data, data.encryptedPan.iv, secretKey);
  const decryptedCVC = await decryptSecret(data.encryptedCvc.data, data.encryptedCvc.iv, secretKey);

  console.log("Decrypted Card Number:", decryptedCardNumber);
  console.log("Decrypted CVC:", decryptedCVC);
  ```
</CodeGroup>


# Restrictions
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/va-automation-restrictions

Regional, country, and industry restrictions that apply to onramp and offramp automations.

Automations are subject to compliance restrictions based on the individual's or business' region, and for business customers, the industry and activity. If a restriction applies, the automation creation request will be rejected.

## Restricted US States

Automations cannot be created for individuals or businesses located in the following US states:

| State          | Code |
| -------------- | ---- |
| California     | CA   |
| Colorado       | CO   |
| Hawaii         | HI   |
| Indiana        | IN   |
| Massachusetts  | MA   |
| Missouri       | MO   |
| New Jersey     | NJ   |
| New York       | NY   |
| North Carolina | NC   |
| Tennessee      | TN   |
| Texas          | TX   |
| Utah           | UT   |
| Virginia       | VA   |

## Restricted Countries

Automations cannot be created for individuals or businesses located in, or with identity documents issued by, the following countries:

| Country                      | Code |
| ---------------------------- | ---- |
| Afghanistan                  | AF   |
| Algeria                      | DZ   |
| Angola                       | AO   |
| Belarus                      | BY   |
| Bhutan                       | BT   |
| Bulgaria                     | BG   |
| Burkina Faso                 | BF   |
| Burundi                      | BI   |
| Cameroon                     | CM   |
| Central African Republic     | CF   |
| China                        | CN   |
| Croatia                      | HR   |
| Cuba                         | CU   |
| Côte d'Ivoire                | CI   |
| Democratic Republic of Congo | CD   |
| Ethiopia                     | ET   |
| Gaza Strip / West Bank       | PS   |
| Guinea Bissau                | GW   |
| Haiti                        | HT   |
| Hong Kong                    | HK   |
| India                        | IN   |
| Iran                         | IR   |
| Iraq                         | IQ   |
| Israel                       | IL   |
| Kenya                        | KE   |
| Kosovo                       | XK   |
| Lebanon                      | LB   |
| Libya                        | LY   |
| Mali                         | ML   |
| Monaco                       | MC   |
| Montenegro                   | ME   |
| Mozambique                   | MZ   |
| Myanmar                      | MM   |
| Namibia                      | NA   |
| Nepal                        | NP   |
| Nicaragua                    | NI   |
| Niger                        | NE   |
| Nigeria                      | NG   |
| North Korea                  | KP   |
| Pakistan                     | PK   |
| Qatar                        | QA   |
| Russia                       | RU   |
| Serbia                       | RS   |
| Somalia                      | SO   |
| South Sudan                  | SS   |
| Sri Lanka                    | LK   |
| Sudan                        | SD   |
| Syria                        | SY   |
| Tanzania                     | TZ   |
| Turkey                       | TR   |
| Ukraine                      | UA   |
| Venezuela                    | VE   |
| Vietnam                      | VN   |
| Yemen                        | YE   |

## Restricted Industries

For business accounts, Automations cannot be created if the company operates in the following industries (by NAICS code):

| Industry                                                     | NAICS Code |
| ------------------------------------------------------------ | ---------- |
| Gambling (casinos)                                           | 713210     |
| Gambling (other)                                             | 713290     |
| Credit repair services                                       | 561450     |
| Credit repair services (other professional)                  | 541990     |
| Money services businesses (financial transaction processing) | 522320     |
| Money services businesses (other)                            | 522390     |
| Pharmaceutical manufacturing (medicinal/botanical)           | 325411     |
| Pharmaceutical manufacturing (in-vitro diagnostics)          | 325412     |
| Weapons / arms manufacturing                                 | 332994     |
| Ammunition manufacturing (small arms)                        | 332992     |
| Ammunition manufacturing (other)                             | 332993     |
| Explosives / fireworks                                       | 325998     |
| Abortion-related services                                    | 621410     |
| Coins, precious metals, gems (wholesale)                     | 423940     |
| Coins, precious metals, gems (retail)                        | 453310     |
| Foreign governments (executive offices)                      | 921110     |
| Foreign governments (other)                                  | 921190     |
| Political action groups                                      | 813940     |
| Embassies / consulates                                       | 928120     |


# Verification and Compliance
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/verification-and-compliance

This page answers common questions related to identity verification, user rejections, AML flags, and compliance documentation for onboarding users in Rain.

<Info>
  ### Can I upload Chinese text when filling out information?

  Currently, only **English** and **Spanish** are supported when filling out text fields.

  However, **documents** can be uploaded in any native language, including Chinese.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### Can Rain’s KYC process integrate with external providers like Persona?

  Yes. You can configure external KYC providers via the Rain Dashboard: Navigate to **Config > External Integrations** to complete setup.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### Can the website field be made optional in corporate applications?

  Yes. If a company does not have a website, enter `N/A` and upload alternate documents, such as:

  * Service contracts
  * Business descriptions
  * Partnership documentation
  * Company descriptions
</Info>

<Info>
  ### How can I change a user’s card status from "needs information" or "not started"?

  * **Needs Information**: Ensure the submitted KYC documents are clear, complete, and legible.
  * **Not Started**: Resubmit the request with all required fields and documents.

  If the issue persists, tag **@RainSupport** in Slack and include the User ID for assistance.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### How is identity verification handled?

  Rain uses a liveness check to verify identity.

  A selfie upload is not sufficient. The liveness check is mandatory and must be passed to complete verification.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### Is there a limit to the number of users that can be created in the production environment?

  There is no limit to the number of users you can create in Rain’s production environment. During production testing, the system enforces a limit of 15 users.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What is the difference between Corporate Structure and Organizational Chart?

  * **Corporate Structure** refers to the hierarchy of legal entities, including parent companies and subsidiaries.
  * **Organizational Chart** illustrates employee roles and reporting lines within a company.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What KYC documents are required?

  **For individuals**:

  * Full name
  * Date of birth
  * ID number
  * Country and address
  * IP address

  **For companies**:

  * Legal name
  * Tax identification number
  * Business description
  * Ultimate Beneficial Owners (UBOs)

  Supporting documents may include:

  * Certificate of incorporation
  * Shareholder register
  * Government-issued ID documents
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What should I do if identity verification fails during account creation?

  Identity verification may fail due to mismatches in submitted documents (e.g., a passport issued from a different country).

  If verification isn’t auto-resolved, Rain’s team will manually review the documents after submission.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What should I do if an application remains pending?

  Make sure that:

  * Clear front and back images of the ID are uploaded
  * You’ve allowed up to 5 business days for processing

  If still pending after that period, contact via **@RainSupport**.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### What should I do if I can't open the application link in production?

  If the application completion link fails to open in production, it's typically due to a missing or incorrect user ID.

  Ensure you are using the correct user ID and that the link matches the expected format.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### Why are users rejected for AML matches?

  If a user's name matches an Anti-Money Laundering (AML) database, the system automatically flags the application.

  **Tip**: Always use the user's full legal name to reduce false positive matches. Avoid using initials or nicknames.
</Info>

<Info>
  ### Why does KYC show `needs more information` after submitting documents?

  This status typically results from:

  * A mismatch between the profile and ID (e.g., date of birth inconsistencies)
  * Poor quality or unreadable uploads

  Submit corrected information via the API. If unresolved, contact Rain via **@RainSupport**.
</Info>


# Verification Troubleshooting
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/verification-troubleshooting

Common issues and solutions for the Sumsub identity verification flow, including camera errors, iframe permissions, and SDK integration problems.

This page covers common issues your users may encounter during the Sumsub identity verification flow and how to resolve them.

## Camera access denied in the Sumsub iframe

If your users see the following error during the selfie or liveness check step:

```
camera-error: Camera access was rejected, reason: "NotAllowedError: Permission denied".
```

This means the browser is blocking camera access inside the Sumsub verification iframe. The parent page must explicitly grant camera permissions to the iframe.

**Fix**: Add the `allow` attribute to the `<iframe>` element that hosts the Sumsub WebSDK:

```html theme={null}
<iframe
  src="..."
  allow="camera; microphone"
></iframe>
```

If you are initializing the Sumsub WebSDK via JavaScript rather than a raw iframe, ensure the SDK's generated iframe inherits these permissions. Most recent versions of the Sumsub WebSDK set this automatically, but older versions or custom iframe wrappers may not.

**Additional checks**:

* The page must be served over **HTTPS**. Browsers block camera access on insecure origins.
* Verify that your server's `Permissions-Policy` HTTP header is not blocking `camera` for the iframe origin.
* On mobile devices, confirm the app or browser has camera permissions granted at the OS level.

## Bypassing verification in sandbox

In the sandbox environment, you can skip the full verification flow by including the desired application status in the user's last name. For example, setting the last name to `TestApproved` will automatically approve the application.

```json theme={null}
{
  "firstName": "John",
  "lastName": "TestApproved",
  "email": "john@example.com",
  "birthDate": "1990-01-01",
  "nationalId": "123456789",
  "countryOfIssue": "US",
  "ipAddress": "127.0.0.1",
  "address": {
    "line1": "123 Main St",
    "city": "New York",
    "postalCode": "10001",
    "countryCode": "US"
  }
}
```

See [Test different application statuses](/docs/signing-up-a-customer#test-different-application-statuses-sandbox-only) for more details and supported status values.


# Viewing Encrypted Card Details
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/viewing-encrypted-card-details

For security and compliance reasons, full card details (card number and CVC) are encrypted and should only be accessed when necessary. While the last four digits and expiry date are always accessible, full card details require a secure retrieval process.

You can allow users to [retrieve encrypted card details](/reference/cards/get-a-cards-encrypted-data), but this requires an encrypted `SessionId` header to ensure only the authorized user can access the data. This guide explains how to generate the session ID, retrieve the encrypted data, and decrypt it securely.

<Warning>
  ### Security best practices

  * Never store decrypted card details.
  * Only request full card details when absolutely necessary.
  * Always use the latest encryption libraries to maintain security.
</Warning>

## Retrieving encrypted card details

There are three different ways to retrieve encrypted card details securely, but they all follow these steps:

1. **Generate a session ID**: Encrypt a secret using the public key.
2. **Send an API request**: Include the encrypted session ID in the request header.
3. **Decrypt the response**: Use the secret key to decrypt the returned card details.

In the following pages, you can find a detailed guide for retrieving encrypted card details through three different ways:

* [Manually implementing encryption and decryption](/docs/manually-implementing-encryption-and-decryption)
* [Using encryption outside of a browser environment](/docs/using-encryption-outside-of-a-browser-environment)


# Managing Transactions
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/viewing-transactions

To monitor and manage transactions efficiently, you can retrieve transaction data, add memos, and attach receipts using the available API endpoints.

## Retrieving transactions

For a dashboard-like view, you can [fetch transactions](/reference/transactions/get-all-transactions) by company, user, or card ID. The response includes:

* **Cursor-based pagination** for handling large datasets.
* **Transaction types** categorized by a `type` field.
* **Structured schemas** for different transaction types.

This allows for efficient querying and filtering of transaction records.

### Transaction Types

You can [fetch transactions](/reference/transactions/get-all-transactions) by type as well. Use the `type` param and set below types:

`Fees` - refers to the fees charged through the Rain [Charge User API](/reference/users/charge-a-user-a-custom-fee) or [Charge Company API](/reference/companies/charge-a-company-a-custom-fee)

`Spend` - refers to regular spend transactions of the cardholder.

`Collateral` - refers to transactions related to the user's or company's collateral contract.

`Payment` - refers to payments made (usually via Liquidation event) against the posted balance of the user or company.

## Adding a memo to a transaction

To store additional context about a transaction, update it by passing a value to the `memo` field.

### Example: Updating a transaction with a memo

Use the [update transaction](/reference/transactions/update-a-transaction) endpoint to attach a memo. The memo will be stored and returned with the transaction details.

## Managing transaction receipts

For `spend` transactions, you can attach and retrieve receipts to enhance record-keeping.

### Attaching a receipt

* Use the [upload receipt](/reference/transactions/upload-a-transactions-receipt) endpoint.
* Send the receipt file as a `multipart/form-data` payload under the `receipt` key.

### Fetching a receipt

* Call the [get transaction receipt](/reference/transactions/get-a-transactions-receipt) endpoint.
* The receipt will be returned as an `application/octet-stream`.


# Overview
Source: https://docs.rain.xyz/docs/webhooks-overview

Webhooks enable real-time event notifications, allowing you to integrate external systems with Rain.

## Setting up webhooks

To receive webhooks, add a webhook URL in your [developer dashboard](/docs/developer-dashboard). Once configured, Rain will send webhook events to the specified URL whenever relevant actions occur.

## Webhook security and signing

Each webhook request is signed using an HMAC SHA256 signature, based on the exact JSON payload sent in the body. This signature is included in the `Signature` HTTP header of the request.

The secret key used to compute the signature is your latest **API key**, which is visible in the Webhooks section of your sandbox or production dashboard.

You can verify webhook authenticity by computing the HMAC signature and comparing it to the `Signature` header included in the webhook request.

### Example: Verifying a webhook signature (Node.js)

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash bash theme={null}
  import { createHmac } from "crypto";

  const signature = createHmac("sha256", <YOUR_LATEST_API_KEY>)
    .update(<REQUEST_BODY_AS_JSON_STRING>) // JSON.stringify(payload)
    .digest("hex");
  ```
</CodeGroup>

Ensure that the computed signature matches the `Signature` header received in the webhook request before processing the payload.

### Best practices

* Return a JSON response with a `200` status code to acknowledge successful receipt
* Include relevant information in your response if you need to communicate back to Rain (e.g., rejection codes for authorization webhooks)
* Avoid returning HTML error pages or plain text responses, as these will be truncated. Otherwise, the response will be stored in the following format:

<CodeGroup>
  ```json Truncated Response Example theme={null}
  {
    "truncated": true,
    "contentType": "text/html",
    "content": "<!DOCTYPE html><html><head><title>Error 500</title></head><body><h1>Internal Server Error</h1><p>An unexpected error occurred while processing your request. Please contact support if this issue persists.</p><div class=\"error-details\"><span>Error ID: abc123</span><span>Timestamp: 2025-12-16T10:30:00Z</span></div></body></html>... [truncated at 1000 characters]"
  }
  ```
</CodeGroup>
